title: The Sociological Trap: Why Popular Investment Theses in Healthcare and Education Keep Failing

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
Healthcare and education are two of the most visibly broken sectors in America. Costs spiral upward, outcomes stagnate or decline, and nearly everyone agrees that fundamental reform is overdue. This combination of obvious dysfunction and moral urgency makes them irresistible to venture capitalists — and that's exactly why they're such treacherous investment categories.

Peter Thiel, drawing on two decades of venture investing through Founders Fund, identifies healthcare IT and education software as the two areas where his returns have been worst. The problem isn't that these sectors lack promising ideas. It's that they attract investors for sociological reasons rather than structural ones. VCs want to brag about their healthcare portfolio at networking events. The moral halo around "fixing education" generates enthusiasm that substitutes for rigorous analysis of whether the political and regulatory barriers are actually surmountable.

Healthcare illustrates the trap perfectly. The payment system is a byzantine web connecting insurance companies, government programs, hospitals, and patients. Any reform must navigate all of these stakeholders simultaneously. Consumers and doctors — the people you'd most like to empower — are typically not the ones writing the checks. Entrepreneurs arrive with what sounds like a clever angle on the pricing model, only to discover ten interlocking barriers they hadn't anticipated. The system isn't just broken; it's architected to resist repair.

Thiel draws a sharp contrast with sectors like defense technology or tunneling, where the binding constraint is political rather than scientific. When Palantir entered military software or SpaceX entered launch services, there were virtually no competitors willing to endure the pain of breaking through. In healthcare and education, the opposite is true: the field is crowded with well-intentioned startups pursuing conventional approaches, none of which can generate the monopoly-like returns that justify the decade of grinding required.

The deeper lesson is about mimetic desire in investing. When an investment thesis is popular — when it feels virtuous, when everyone at the conference is nodding along — that popularity itself is a warning signal. The best opportunities look insane to most people, which is precisely why they offer no competition and enormous upside if you can break through.
